Case title: Lab manuscript co-writing with version histories and authorship disclosure
Short description: Track how AI-assisted drafting redistributes visible and invisible labor across PIs, doctoral students, and lab managers.
Empirical unit of analysis: Version histories, AI suggestion logs, authorship statements, and reviewer reactions
Observable human actors: PI, doctoral student, lab manager, reviewer
Candidate roles tested: Accountability and labor allocator; shrinking structural prose polisher
Key typology question: Does AI shift human work from drafting to curation and credit allocation, and does that alter evaluation?
Observable artifacts / traces: Version histories, suggestion uptake, authorship disclosures, reviewer vignette responses
Key outcomes observed: Perceived polish, perceived originality, credit allocation, evaluation effects
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI drafting neither redistributes credit nor changes evaluation after quality is controlled
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Pre-existing hierarchy may explain much of the credit allocation
Why this case has high leverage: It makes hidden labor and ownership visible instead of assuming them.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
